segments.title: Cutty Sark's rigging reveals clipper complexity
segments.summary: Aboard Cutty Sark, the preserved rigging makes the complexity of a fast sailing ship visible before the visit turns to the trade she served.
segments.body: The visit begins aboard Cutty Sark, where Dr. Clarke asks the camera to turn toward the rigging. The dense web of lines lets viewers see complexity that a short verbal description of a sailing clipper can hide. His reaction is deliberately immediate: the surviving arrangement makes the ship's technical achievement feel tangible. This brief opening provides a visual orientation before the discussion shifts from the ship's fabric to the cargo system that made fast clippers commercially useful.

evidence.note: Aboard Cutty Sark, Dr. Clarke directs attention to the visible rigging and says it reveals how remarkable these ships were.

segments.title: Tea chests standardized the clipper trade
segments.summary: Tea chests provide a compact lesson in how standardized cargo packaging supported the fast, large-scale trade served by Cutty Sark.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames tea chests as an early form of globally standardized transport packaging. He contrasts their uniform boxes with modern intermodal containers and with wool, which was standardized through wrapping rather than boxed units. Repeatable packages made it possible to organize and move goods at large scale, linking cargo handling to the operation of the trade. Cutty Sark and other tea clippers were built around speed, so the watch point connects the physical ship to the commercial system it served. The clip presents this as a concise historical analogy rather than a detailed account of how modern containerization developed.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke identifies tea chests as an early standardized transport box and contrasts them with modern intermodal containers and wrapped wool.
He links mass-scale standardized goods transport to the speed-focused role of tea clippers.
